lessons.title: Read Genesis 1:1–13
lessons.reflectPrompt: What does it mean that God called the light 'good' before there was anyone to see it? What does that say about how God works?

historicalContext.summary: Ancient Near Eastern cultures (Babylonian, Egyptian, Canaanite) each had elaborate creation myths. The Babylonian Enuma Elish describes creation as a violent battle between gods, with humanity created from a slain god's blood to be servants of the gods. Genesis stands apart: one God creates by speech alone, repeatedly calls the result 'good,' and makes humanity in His own image — not as slaves but as bearers of dignity.
historicalContext.believed: Traditional Jewish and Christian scholarship attributes the Pentateuch to Moses (~1450 BC). Many modern critical scholars place its final compilation later (6th–5th century BC). Both views agree Genesis stands in deliberate contrast to surrounding creation myths.
sources: Walton, The Lost World of Genesis One

questions.prompt: On which day did God create light?
questions.explain: Light comes on Day 1; the sun, moon, and stars not until Day 4 — a deliberate order.

questions.prompt: What did God create on the sixth day?
questions.explain: Land animals and then humanity — male and female in God's image.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'In the beginning God created the ____ and the earth.'
What did God do on the seventh day?
questions.explain: He rested and blessed the seventh day, setting the pattern of Sabbath.

checkQuestion.prompt: What sets Genesis apart from other ancient creation accounts?
options: It describes creation as a battle between gods
One God creates by word and calls it good
checkQuestion.explain: Genesis depicts a single God speaking creation into being and pronouncing it good — a sharp contrast to violent creation myths of the region.

lessons.title: Memorize Genesis 1:1
lessons.text: In the beginning God created the heaven and the earth.

lessons.title: Read Genesis 1:26–2:3
lessons.reflectPrompt: What does it mean to be made in the image of God? How might that shape how you see other people?

historicalContext.timeframe: Day 6 of the Genesis creation account
historicalContext.summary: In the ancient Near East, only kings were said to bear a god's image — typically through carved stone statues placed in temples to represent the god's rule over a city. Genesis 1:26-27 takes this royal language and democratizes it radically: every human, male AND female, bears the image of God. There is no precedent for this in surrounding cultures.

questions.prompt: In whose image was humanity made?
What dominion was given to humanity?
options: Over fish, fowl, and every living thing

questions.prompt: 'So God created man in his own ____'

lessons.title: Read Genesis 2:7–25
lessons.reflectPrompt: How does God's care in shaping Adam and Eve contrast with the broad strokes of Genesis 1?

historicalContext.location: Garden of Eden — the text places it 'eastward,' with rivers including the Tigris and Euphrates
historicalContext.summary: Genesis 2 zooms in from the cosmic sweep of chapter 1 to an intimate portrait: God forming Adam from dust and breathing life into him. The four-river description corresponds to Mesopotamian geography (the Tigris and Euphrates are real, identifiable rivers). In the ancient Near East, gardens were a royal/divine motif — kings often built sacred gardens near temples.
historicalContext.believed: Most scholars treat Eden as a theological landscape rather than a precisely locatable place. Proposed locations include parts of modern Iraq, Iran, Turkey, and even the now-flooded Persian Gulf basin, but none are confirmed.

lessons.title: Read Genesis 3
lessons.reflectPrompt: Where does the serpent's deception begin — with the action, or with how Eve sees God? What's the pattern?

historicalContext.summary: The Hebrew word for 'serpent' (nachash) carried double meaning in ancient Near Eastern culture — associated with both wisdom and danger, sometimes with divinity itself (serpent images appear in Egyptian, Canaanite, and Mesopotamian religious art). Genesis 3:15 — the promise that the woman's seed will bruise the serpent's head — is traditionally called the protoevangelium, the 'first gospel,' because Christian readers have always seen it as the earliest hint of a coming Redeemer.

questions.prompt: What did the serpent say would happen if Eve ate the fruit?
options: Her eyes would be opened and she'd be as gods
She would have to leave the garden

questions.prompt: Who is the 'seed' in Genesis 3:15 that will bruise the serpent's head?
options: A future descendant — traditionally read as the Messiah
questions.explain: Genesis 3:15 is often called the protoevangelium — the first hint of the gospel.

questions.prompt: Put these events in order:
items: Eve eats the fruit and gives to Adam
Their eyes are opened and they hide
God pronounces judgment and the promise of a seed

lessons.title: Read Genesis 6:5–22
lessons.reflectPrompt: What did Noah find that the rest of the world did not? What does that suggest about how God evaluates a generation?

historicalContext.summary: Nearly every ancient civilization preserves a flood narrative — the Babylonian Epic of Gilgamesh (Tablet 11) describes a flood survivor named Utnapishtim who builds a boat at the gods' warning. Similar accounts come from Greek, Chinese, Indian, and Mesoamerican traditions. What sets Genesis apart is its moral framing: the flood is God's response to human wickedness, ending in a covenant of grace symbolized by the rainbow.
historicalContext.believed: Scholars debate whether the flood was global or a major regional event. Some geologists have proposed the catastrophic flooding of the Black Sea basin (around 5600 BC) as a possible memory source for the various flood traditions, though this is not universally accepted.
sources: Walton & Longman, The Lost World of the Flood

questions.prompt: How many of each clean animal did Noah take into the ark?
questions.explain: Genesis 7:2 — by sevens of clean beasts, by twos of unclean.

questions.prompt: What sign did God give as a covenant after the flood?
'And Noah found ____ in the eyes of the LORD.'

lessons.title: Read Genesis 12:1–9
lessons.reflectPrompt: What did Abraham have to leave behind? What was he promised in exchange?

historicalContext.location: Ur of the Chaldees → Haran → Canaan
historicalContext.summary: Ur was a major Sumerian city near the Persian Gulf, prosperous in the early second millennium BC, with stone temples (ziggurats), libraries of cuneiform tablets, and trade networks reaching the Indus Valley. Haran was an important caravan hub in northern Mesopotamia, on a trade route between Mesopotamia and Anatolia. Abraham left an urban, literate civilization for a pastoral life among Canaanite city-states.
historicalContext.believed: Many historians accept the patriarchal narratives as preserving genuine second-millennium-BC customs (adoption laws, household practices, naming conventions) even where exact dates are debated. Some critical scholars treat Abraham as a literary/legendary figure rather than strictly historical.
sources: Kitchen, On the Reliability of the Old Testament

checkQuestion.prompt: What was Ur of the Chaldees?

questions.prompt: How old was Abraham when God called him?
What was God's promise to Abraham?

lessons.title: Read Genesis 22:1–14
lessons.reflectPrompt: What does Abraham's willingness reveal about his trust in God? What does the substitute ram foreshadow?

historicalContext.location: Mount Moriah — traditionally identified with the Temple Mount in Jerusalem (2 Chronicles 3:1)
historicalContext.summary: Surrounding Canaanite cultures practiced child sacrifice to deities like Molech — this is archaeologically attested at Phoenician colonial sites like Carthage, where thousands of infant urns have been excavated in dedicated tophet precincts. Yahweh's command appeared to fit that horrifying pattern — then dramatically reversed it. The ram caught in the thicket made a permanent theological statement: the God of Israel does not require human sacrifice.
sources: Levenson, The Death and Resurrection of the Beloved Son

questions.prompt: What did God provide in place of Isaac?
options: A ram caught in the thicket

questions.prompt: Abraham called the place 'Jehovahjireh' — meaning 'the LORD will ____'.

lessons.title: Read Genesis 50:15–21
lessons.reflectPrompt: 'Ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good.' How does Joseph's perspective on suffering reframe his story?

historicalContext.summary: During the Hyksos period (15th–17th Dynasties, c. 1650–1550 BC), Semitic peoples held significant administrative power in Egypt. Egyptian records describe officials with Semitic names rising to vizier-level positions — exactly Joseph's role. The seven-year famine described in Genesis fits Egypt's known dependence on the Nile flooding; failed inundations did cause multi-year famines.
historicalContext.believed: Joseph's exact timeline is debated. The Hyksos era is the most commonly proposed setting because it offers the best historical explanation for how a foreign Semite could rise to 'second only to Pharaoh.'

questions.prompt: What did Joseph's brothers do to him?
options: Sold him into slavery in Egypt

questions.prompt: What position did Joseph eventually hold?

lessons.title: Read Exodus 3:1–14
lessons.reflectPrompt: What name does God give Himself, and why does it matter that He defines Himself this way?

historicalContext.timeframe: ~1450 BC (traditional early date) or ~1290 BC (late date)
historicalContext.location: Wilderness near Mount Horeb/Sinai — traditionally the southern Sinai peninsula
historicalContext.summary: Moses had fled Egypt 40 years earlier after killing an Egyptian taskmaster and was shepherding his father-in-law Jethro's flocks in Midian (northwest Arabia). The divine name 'I AM' (Hebrew YHWH) given here is the personal covenant name of God — Israel considered it so holy that they would not pronounce it aloud, substituting 'Adonai' (Lord) when reading scripture.
historicalContext.believed: The 'early date' (~1450 BC) follows 1 Kings 6:1 and is held by traditional scholars. Many critical scholars prefer ~1290 BC under Pharaoh Ramses II based on archaeological evidence. The exact location of Mount Sinai is uncertain — at least a dozen mountains have been proposed.

checkQuestion.prompt: Each plague was a strike against what?

lessons.title: Read Exodus 12:1–14
lessons.reflectPrompt: Why does God establish a memorial meal? What does it teach about how He wants His people to remember?

historicalContext.timeframe: Night of the tenth plague, ~1450 BC (early date) or ~1290 BC (late date)
historicalContext.summary: Passover is the founding meal of Israel's national identity. Lamb's blood applied to doorposts and lintel marked Israelite homes — the destroyer 'passed over' them during the tenth plague. The meal was eaten in haste (unleavened bread, sandals on, staff in hand) — preparing for immediate departure. Jews have continued to celebrate Passover annually for over 3,400 years, making it one of the longest-running religious observances in human history.

questions.prompt: What sign protected Israelite homes during the final plague?
How did Israel cross the Red Sea?

lessons.title: Read Joshua 6:1–20
lessons.reflectPrompt: What was God teaching Israel by making them march in silence for six days?

historicalContext.location: Jericho — ~6 miles north of the Dead Sea, near a perennial spring
historicalContext.summary: Jericho is one of the oldest continuously-inhabited places on earth, with archaeological layers going back to ~9000 BC. Excavations have confirmed it was a substantial walled city with massive stone retaining walls and mud-brick upper walls. A destruction layer exists where the walls of the city fell outward — unusual, since walls typically fall inward when attacked from outside.
historicalContext.believed: Archaeologists Bryant Wood and others date the destruction layer to ~1400 BC, matching the biblical account. Kathleen Kenyon's earlier excavations dated it to ~1550 BC, which would predate the conquest — this dating remains debated. Both groups agree the city was destroyed and its walls collapsed.
sources: Wood, Did the Israelites Conquer Jericho? (Biblical Archaeology Review)

lessons.title: Memorize Joshua 1:9
lessons.text: Have not I commanded thee? Be strong and of a good courage; be not afraid, neither be thou dismayed: for the LORD thy God is with thee whithersoever thou goest.

lessons.title: Read Ruth 1:8–18
lessons.reflectPrompt: Why does Ruth's choice to follow Naomi matter for the story of redemption?

historicalContext.timeframe: ~1100 BC, during the period of the Judges
historicalContext.location: Moab (east of the Dead Sea) → Bethlehem (in Judah)
historicalContext.summary: Moabites were descendants of Lot (Genesis 19) and longstanding enemies of Israel — Deuteronomy 23:3 specifically banned them from full membership in the assembly of Israel. Ruth's loyalty to her Israelite mother-in-law and her conversion to Yahweh's worship crossed deep ethnic and religious lines. The book's punch line is in 4:17: Ruth becomes the great-grandmother of King David — and thus is named in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:5).

questions.prompt: Who was Ruth's great-grandson?
questions.explain: Ruth's loyalty places her in the genealogy of David — and ultimately Jesus.

units.title: Kings & Prophets
units.subtitle: Unit 5 · From David to the Exile

lessons.title: Read 1 Samuel 17:32–50
lessons.reflectPrompt: What made David confident against Goliath? What does that teach about whose fight it really was?

historicalContext.location: Valley of Elah — ~15 miles west of Jerusalem in the Shephelah foothills
historicalContext.summary: The Philistines were a 'Sea People' who settled the southern coast of Canaan around 1175 BC, originating from the Aegean (possibly Crete). They brought iron-working technology that gave them a military edge over the Israelites, who were still using bronze. Their pentapolis (five cities: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron, Gath — Goliath's hometown) dominated the coastal plain.
historicalContext.believed: Goliath's height is given as 'six cubits and a span' (~9'9') in the traditional Hebrew text. The earliest manuscripts (Dead Sea Scrolls fragment 4QSam-a and the Greek Septuagint) read 'four cubits and a span' (~6'9'), which some scholars consider the more original reading. Both readings describe a remarkably tall warrior.

checkQuestion.prompt: Why was Jerusalem strategic as a capital?
options: It was geographically neutral and outside any tribe

lessons.title: Memorize Psalm 23:1
lessons.text: The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.

lessons.title: Read Daniel 1:8–17
lessons.reflectPrompt: Daniel 'purposed in his heart' before pressure arrived. What does that teach about preparation?

historicalContext.timeframe: 605 BC, the first wave of Babylonian deportation
historicalContext.location: Babylon — Nebuchadnezzar's capital, then the most powerful city on earth
historicalContext.summary: Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar II was the largest city in the world (perhaps 200,000 people) with the famous Ishtar Gate, the Hanging Gardens (one of the seven ancient wonders), and the great ziggurat. The royal training program described in Daniel 1 was a deliberate assimilation strategy: change the captives' language, names, diet, and religion to break their original identity. Daniel and his three friends refused the king's food — likely because it had been offered to Babylonian gods and violated Mosaic dietary laws.

checkQuestion.prompt: When did Babylon destroy the Temple?

lessons.title: Read John 1:1–14
lessons.reflectPrompt: What does it mean that the Word was both 'with God' and 'was God'? How does v.14 change everything?

historicalContext.timeframe: Written ~85–95 AD; describes events ~4 BC–30 AD
historicalContext.location: Composed in Ephesus (per early church tradition); describes events in Galilee and Judea
historicalContext.summary: John wrote to a mixed audience of Jews and Greeks at the end of the first century. The Greek word 'Logos' (Word) carried weight in both worlds: Jewish thinkers heard echoes of God's creative speech in Genesis 1 ('And God said…'), while Greek philosophers (especially Stoics) used Logos for the rational principle that orders the universe. John takes both threads and weaves them into a single claim: this Word is Jesus, and He became flesh and lived among us.
historicalContext.believed: Most scholars accept Johannine authorship of the fourth gospel, written by the apostle John in his old age (late 1st century). A minority assigns it to a 'Johannine community' or anonymous author. The Ephesus tradition comes from Irenaeus (~180 AD).

checkQuestion.prompt: Which group did NOT exist in first-century Judea?

lessons.title: Memorize John 3:16
lessons.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life.

lessons.title: Read Luke 24:1–12
lessons.reflectPrompt: Why did the women's report sound like 'idle tales' to the disciples? What changed?

historicalContext.location: A tomb outside Jerusalem — traditionally identified with the area now occupied by the Church of the Holy Sepulchre
historicalContext.summary: In first-century Jewish and Roman culture, women's testimony was not legally admissible in formal court proceedings — Josephus explicitly notes this exclusion. That all four gospels record women as the FIRST witnesses to the empty tomb is striking. No first-century fabricator inventing a religion would have made women the primary witnesses if they were free to choose otherwise — which is itself a small but real argument for the accounts' historical core.
sources: Bauckham, Gospel Women; Wright, The Resurrection of the Son of God

lessons.title: Read Acts 2:1–21
lessons.reflectPrompt: Why did God choose languages — many languages — as the first sign of the Spirit's coming?

historicalContext.summary: Pentecost (Greek 'fiftieth') was one of three pilgrimage festivals when Jews from across the Roman Empire traveled to Jerusalem. The city's population swelled dramatically during the holiday. Acts 2 specifically lists pilgrims from 15+ regions — Parthia, Media, Mesopotamia, Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia, Phrygia, Pamphylia, Egypt, Libya, Rome, Crete, Arabia, and others. When the disciples spoke in languages they hadn't learned, pilgrims from each region heard the gospel in their own dialect.

questions.prompt: What appeared above the disciples at Pentecost?
options: Cloven tongues like as of fire

lessons.title: Read Romans 5:1–11
lessons.reflectPrompt: What is the connection between justification, peace with God, and rejoicing in tribulation?

historicalContext.location: Written from Corinth to the church in Rome
historicalContext.summary: Paul wrote Romans to a church he hadn't personally founded or yet visited — he wanted to introduce his gospel before arriving (he was on his way to Spain via Rome). Rome at this time had ~1 million inhabitants and was the largest city in the world. The church there was a mix of Jewish and Gentile believers, with tensions between them — Paul writes Romans partly to demonstrate that the gospel works the same for both.

checkQuestion.prompt: What gave the first-century church an infrastructure advantage?
options: Roman roads, Greek language, and Mediterranean shipping

lessons.title: Memorize Romans 8:28
lessons.text: And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose.

units.title: Revelation & Promises Fulfilled
units.subtitle: Unit 10 · The end of the story

lessons.title: Read Revelation 21:1–7
lessons.reflectPrompt: Compare this to Genesis 1–3. What is restored? What is new?

historicalContext.location: Patmos — a small rocky Roman penal island in the Aegean Sea, off the coast of modern-day Turkey
historicalContext.summary: John wrote Revelation while exiled to Patmos under the Roman emperor Domitian, who demanded to be addressed as 'Lord and God' (Dominus et Deus) and began persecuting Christians who refused to participate in emperor worship. The vision of the New Jerusalem deliberately reverses Genesis 3: the curse is undone, the tree of life returns, God dwells with humanity again — the entire arc of scripture completes itself.
sources: Bauckham, The Theology of the Book of Revelation

questions.prompt: In the New Jerusalem, what will God wipe from every eye?
'Behold, I make all things ____.'
